In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-
ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19])
without modifying the solvers.
We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet
[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-
sults substantially better than previous networks.
Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-
layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on
ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG
nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on theImageNet testset, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC
2015 classiﬁcation competition. The extremely deep rep-
resentations also have excellent generalization performance
on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the
1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization,
COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].
These gates are data-dependent and have parameters, in
contrast to our identity shortcuts that are parameter-free.
When a gated shortcut is “closed” (approaching zero), the
layers in highway networks represent non-residual func-
tions. On the contrary, our formulation always learns
residual functions; our identity shortcuts are never closed,
and all information is always passed through, with addi-
tional residual functions to be learned. In addition, high-
2', 'way networks have not demonstrated accuracy gains with
extremely increased depth ( e.g., over 100 layers).
3. Deep Residual Learning
3.1. Residual Learning
Let us considerH(x)as an underlying mapping to be
ﬁt by a few stacked layers (not necessarily the entire net),
withxdenoting the inputs to the ﬁrst of these layers. If one
hypothesizes that multiple nonlinear layers can asymptoti-
cally approximate complicated functions2, then it is equiv-
alent to hypothesize that they can asymptotically approxi-
mate the residual functions, i.e.,H(x)\x00x(assuming that
the input and output are of the same dimensions). So
rather than expect stacked layers to approximate H(x), we
explicitly let these layers approximate a residual function
F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The original function thus becomes
F(x)+x. Although both forms should be able to asymptot-
ically approximate the desired functions (as hypothesized),
the ease of learning might be different.
This reformulation is motivated by the counterintuitive
phenomena about the degradation problem (Fig. 1, left). As
we discussed in the introduction, if the added layers can
be constructed as identity mappings, a deeper model should
have training error no greater than its shallower counter-
part. The degradation problem suggests that the solvers
might have difﬁculties in approximating identity mappings
by multiple nonlinear layers. With the residual learning re-
formulation, if identity mappings are optimal, the solvers
may simply drive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay-
ers toward zero to approach identity mappings.
In real cases, it is unlikely that identity mappings are op-
timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the
problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity
mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the
solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity
mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show
by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual functions in
general have small responses, suggesting that identity map-
pings provide reasonable preconditioning.
3.2. Identity Mapping by Shortcuts
We adopt residual learning to every few stacked layers.
A building block is shown in Fig. 2. Formally, in this paper
we consider a building block deﬁned as:
y=F(x;fWig) +x: (1)
Here xandyare the input and output vectors of the lay-
ers considered. The function F(x;fWig)represents the
residual mapping to be learned. For the example in Fig. 2
that has two layers, F=W2\x1b(W1x)in which\x1bdenotes
2This hypothesis, however, is still an open question. See [28].ReLU [29] and the biases are omitted for simplifying no-
tations. The operation F+xis performed by a shortcut
connection and element-wise addition. We adopt the sec-

The shortcut connections in Eqn.(1) introduce neither ex-
tra parameter nor computation complexity. This is not only
attractive in practice but also important in our comparisons
between plain and residual networks. We can fairly com-
pare plain/residual networks that simultaneously have the
same number of parameters, depth, width, and computa-
tional cost (except for the negligible element-wise addition).
The dimensions of xandFmust be equal in Eqn.(1).
If this is not the case ( e.g., when changing the input/output
channels), we can perform a linear projection Wsby the
shortcut connections to match the dimensions:
y=F(x;fWig) +Wsx: (2)
We can also use a square matrix Wsin Eqn.(1). But we will
show by experiments that the identity mapping is sufﬁcient
for addressing the degradation problem and is economical,
and thusWsis only used when matching dimensions.
The form of the residual function Fis ﬂexible. Exper-
iments in this paper involve a function Fthat has two or
three layers (Fig. 5), while more layers are possible. But if

y=W1x+x, for which we have not observed advantages.
We also note that although the above notations are about
fully-connected layers for simplicity, they are applicable to
convolutional layers. The function F(x;fWig)can repre-
sent multiple convolutional layers. The element-wise addi-
tion is performed on two feature maps, channel by channel.
3.3. Network Architectures
We have tested various plain/residual nets, and have ob-
served consistent phenomena. To provide instances for dis-
cussion, we describe two models for ImageNet as follows.
Plain Network. Our plain baselines (Fig. 3, middle) are
mainly inspired by the philosophy of VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3,
left). The convolutional layers mostly have 3 \x023 ﬁlters and
follow two simple design rules: (i) for the same output
feature map size, the layers have the same number of ﬁl-
ters; and (ii) if the feature map size is halved, the num-
ber of ﬁlters is doubled so as to preserve the time com-
plexity per layer. We perform downsampling directly by
convolutional layers that have a stride of 2. The network
ends with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way
fully-connected layer with softmax. The total number of
weighted layers is 34 in Fig. 3 (middle).
It is worth noticing that our model has fewer ﬁlters and
lower complexity than VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3, left). Our 34-
layer baseline has 3.6 billion FLOPs (multiply-adds), which
is only 18% of VGG-19 (19.6 billion FLOPs).

Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.

trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

response:
Residual networks (RNs) are a type of neural network architecture that addresses the degradation problem in deep learning models by incorporating a residual connection between each layer. The residual connection is a shortcut path that bypasses the input feature map and directly passes through the output feature map, thereby reducing the amount of information lost during the forward pass. This reduces the need for backpropagation through the entire network, which can help prevent overfitting and improve generalization performance.

In RNs, each layer in the network has a residual connection between it and the previous layer's output feature map. This means that the input to the next layer is not directly passed through the output of the previous layer, but instead passes through the output of the previous layer plus the input to the current layer. This reduces the amount of information lost during the forward pass and helps prevent overfitting by reducing the need for backpropagation.

Residual networks have been shown to improve generalization performance in a variety of deep learning tasks, including image classification, natural language processing, and speech recognition. They also have the advantage of being easier to train than traditional neural network architectures due to their simpler structure.

This approach results in the 34-layer ResNet exhibiting considerably lower training error and better generalization to validation data compared to its plain counterpart, effectively managing to obtain accuracy gains from increased depth.

3. Deep Residual Learning
3.1. Residual Learning
Let us considerH(x)as an underlying mapping to be
ﬁt by a few stacked layers (not necessarily the entire net),
withxdenoting the inputs to the ﬁrst of these layers. If one
hypothesizes that multiple nonlinear layers can asymptoti-
cally approximate complicated functions2, then it is equiv-
alent to hypothesize that they can asymptotically approxi-
mate the residual functions, i.e.,H(x)\x00x(assuming that
the input and output are of the same dimensions). So
rather than expect stacked layers to approximate H(x), we
explicitly let these layers approximate a residual function
F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The original function thus becomes
F(x)+x. Although both forms should be able to asymptot-
ically approximate the desired functions (as hypothesized),
the ease of learning might be different.
This reformulation is motivated by the counterintuitive
phenomena about the degradation problem (Fig. 1, left). As
we discussed in the introduction, if the added layers can
be constructed as identity mappings, a deeper model should
have training error no greater than its shallower counter-
part. The degradation problem suggests that the solvers
might have difﬁculties in approximating identity mappings
by multiple nonlinear layers. With the residual learning re-
formulation, if identity mappings are optimal, the solvers
may simply drive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay-
ers toward zero to approach identity mappings.

timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the
problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity
mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the
solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity
mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show
by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual functions in
general have small responses, suggesting that identity map-
pings provide reasonable preconditioning.

Identity shortcuts, also known as "bottleneck" layers or "identity maps," contribute to the efficiency of deeper bottleneck architecture (DBA) in ImageNet ResNet models by performing a linear projection on two feature maps, each with a size of 28x28. This reduces the number of parameters required for the network and allows it to perform more complex operations without increasing its complexity. By using identity shortcuts, the DBA can reduce the number of layers in the network while maintaining the same level of performance as a standard ResNet with fewer layers. This is because the bottleneck layer performs a linear projection on two feature maps, each with a size of 28x28, which reduces the number of parameters required for the network and allows it to perform more complex operations without increasing its complexity. In summary, identity shortcuts contribute to the efficiency of deeper DBA in ImageNet ResNet models by reducing the number of layers while maintaining the same level of performance as a standard ResNet with fewer layers.

reference: Identity shortcuts are particularly important for the bottleneck architectures in ImageNet ResNet models because they do not increase the time complexity or model size. In the deeper bottleneck design, which uses a stack of three layers (1×1, 3×3, and 1×1 convolutions), the identity shortcut connects low-dimensional ends without introducing extra parameters. If the identity shortcut were replaced with projection shortcuts, the time complexity and model size would be doubled, as the shortcut would then be connected to the two high-dimensional ends. Therefore, using identity shortcuts leads to more efficient models for the bottleneck architectures.

response:
The question asks about the problem of vanishing/exploding gradient in deep neural networks, which is a common issue that can cause overfitting and degrade accuracy. The answer to this question is that normalized initial-ization (NIO) and intermediate normalization layers (INLs) are used to prevent overfitting and improve the convergence of deeper models with shallower counterparts. These techniques enable networks with tens of layers to start converging for stochastiic gradient descent (SGD), which is not caused by overfitting, but rather by a degradation problem that can be solved by adding more layers onto it. The existence of these solutions indicates that a deeper model should produce no higher training error than its shallower counterpart.

Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
1. Introduction
Deep convolutional neural networks [22, 21] have led
to a series of breakthroughs for image classiﬁcation [21,
50, 40]. Deep networks naturally integrate low/mid/high-
level features [50] and classiﬁers in an end-to-end multi-
layer fashion, and the “levels” of features can be enriched
by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence
[41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance,
and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging
ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models,
with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Many other non-
trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

Driven by the signiﬁcance of depth, a question arises: Is
learning better networks as easy as stacking more layers?
An obstacle to answering this question was the notorious
problem of vanishing/exploding gradients [1, 9], which
hamper convergence from the beginning. This problem,
however, has been largely addressed by normalized initial-
ization [23, 9, 37, 13] and intermediate normalization layers
[16], which enable networks with tens of layers to start con-
verging for stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with back-
propagation [22].
When deeper networks are able to start converging, a
degradation problem has been exposed: with the network
depth increasing, accuracy gets saturated (which might be
unsurprising) and then degrades rapidly. Unexpectedly,
such degradation is not caused by overﬁtting, and adding
more layers to a suitably deep model leads to higher train-
ing error, as reported in [11, 42] and thoroughly veriﬁed by
our experiments. Fig. 1 shows a typical example.
The degradation (of training accuracy) indicates that not
all systems are similarly easy to optimize. Let us consider a
shallower architecture and its deeper counterpart that adds
more layers onto it. There exists a solution by construction
to the deeper model: the added layers are identity mapping,
and the other layers are copied from the learned shallower
model. The existence of this constructed solution indicates
that a deeper model should produce no higher training error
than its shallower counterpart. But experiments show that
our current solvers on hand are unable to ﬁnd solutions that

C. ImageNet Localization
The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to
classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we
assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for
predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza-
tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes
based on the predicted classes. We adopt the “per-class re-
gression” (PCR) strategy [40, 41], learning a bounding box
regressor for each class. We pre-train the networks for Im-
ageNet classiﬁcation and then ﬁne-tune them for localiza-
tion. We train networks on the provided 1000-class Ima-
geNet training set.
Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame-
work of [32] with a few modiﬁcations. Unlike the way in
[32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is
designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib-
ling 1\x021 convolutional layers for binary classiﬁcation ( cls)
and box regression ( reg), as in [32]. The clsandreglayers
are both in a per-class from, in contrast to [32]. Speciﬁ-
cally, the clslayer has a 1000-d output, and each dimension
isbinary logistic regression for predicting being or not be-
ing an object class; the reglayer has a 1000\x024-d output
consisting of box regressors for 1000 classes. As in [32],
our bounding box regression is with reference to multiple
translation-invariant “anchor” boxes at each position.
As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we
randomly sample 224 \x02224 crops for data augmentation.
We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To
avoid negative samples being dominate, 8 anchors are ran-
domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive
and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing,
the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally.

of Fast R-CNN [7] generates samples of small variations,
which may not be desired for stochastic training. Motivated
by this, in our current experiment we use the original R-
CNN [8] that is RoI-centric, in place of Fast R-CNN.
Our R-CNN implementation is as follows. We apply the
per-class RPN trained as above on the training images to
predict bounding boxes for the ground truth class. These
predicted boxes play a role of class-dependent proposals.
For each training image, the highest scored 200 proposals
are extracted as training samples to train an R-CNN classi-
ﬁer. The image region is cropped from a proposal, warped

testing, the RPN generates the highest scored 200 proposals

update these proposals’ scores and box positions.

In the context of CIFAR-10 image classification with data augmentation, Highway networks (i.e., networks that use a combination of convolutional and pooling layers to enhance the receptive field) are reported to perform better than ResNets in terms of classification error, depth, and number of parameters. Specifically, the following results were reported:

1. Classification error: Highway networks outperformed ResNets by 64% relative reduction in error on the ImageNet dataset.

2. Depth: The Highway network with a depth of 50 achieved a top-5 localization error of 9.0%, which is significantly better than the ILSVRC 14 results (Table 14) that were reported to be achieved by ResNets with a depth of 32.

3. Number of parameters: The Highway network with a depth of 50 had fewer number of parameters compared to ResNets, which resulted in a reduction in the overall model's complexity.

Overall, Highway networks are reported to perform better than ResNets in terms of classification error, depth, and number of parameters for image classification with data augmentation.

- ResNet.pdf | sections: References | p. 1, 7, 8, 9, 12
reference:
On the CIFAR-10 test set with data augmentation, Highway networks and ResNets are compared in terms of classification error, depth, and number of parameters as summarized in Table 6.

For Highway networks [42, 43]:
- A 19-layer Highway network with 2.3M parameters achieves an error of 7.54% (reported as 7.54 (7.72±0.16)).
- A 32-layer Highway network with 1.25M parameters achieves an error of 8.80%.

For ResNets:
- ResNet-20 (20 layers, 0.27M parameters) has 8.75% error.
- ResNet-32 (32 layers, 0.46M parameters) has 7.51% error.
- ResNet-44 (44 layers, 0.66M parameters) has 7.17% error.
- ResNet-56 (56 layers, 0.85M parameters) has 6.97% error.
- ResNet-110 (110 layers, 1.7M parameters) has 6.43% error (reported as 6.43 (6.61±0.16) over 5 runs).
- ResNet-1202 (1202 layers, 19.4M parameters) has 7.93% error.

All methods in the table, including Highway networks and ResNets, are evaluated with data augmentation. The table shows that the 19-layer Highway network (2.3M parameters, 7.54% error) is competitive with shallower ResNets, while deeper ResNets (e.g., 56 and 110 layers) achieve lower error rates with fewer or comparable parameters than the reported Highway configurations.

do not use option C in the rest of this paper, to reduce mem-

the bottleneck architectures that are introduced below.
Deeper Bottleneck Architectures. Next we describe our
deeper nets for ImageNet. Because of concerns on the train-
ing time that we can afford, we modify the building block
as a bottleneck design4. For each residual function F, we
use a stack of 3 layers instead of 2 (Fig. 5). The three layers
are 1\x021, 3\x023, and 1\x021 convolutions, where the 1 \x021 layers
are responsible for reducing and then increasing (restoring)
dimensions, leaving the 3 \x023 layer a bottleneck with smaller
input/output dimensions. Fig. 5 shows an example, where
both designs have similar time complexity.
The parameter-free identity shortcuts are particularly im-
portant for the bottleneck architectures. If the identity short-
cut in Fig. 5 (right) is replaced with projection, one can
show that the time complexity and model size are doubled,
as the shortcut is connected to the two high-dimensional
ends. So identity shortcuts lead to more efﬁcient models
for the bottleneck designs.

6', 'We further observe in rows (C) and (D) that, as expected, bigger models are better, and dropout is very helpful in avoiding over-fitting. In row (E) we replace our sinusoidal positional encoding with learned positional embeddings [ 9], and observe nearly identical results to the base model.']
response:
FitNet, a deeper residual network (ResNet) with a bottleneck architecture, outperforms ResNet in terms of network parameters and performance. The bottleneck architecture is particularly important for the ResNet designs as it leads to more efﬁcient models and is not as economical as the deeper non-bottleneck ResNets. FitNet also has a smaller model size compared to ResNet, which is witnessed in rows (C) and (D). Dropout is very helpful in avoiding over-fitting, and replacing the sinusoidal positional encoding with learned positional embedding in row (E) results in nearly identical performance to the base model. Overall, FitNet outperforms ResNet in terms of network parameters and performance, highlighting the importance of using deeper residual networks for image classification tasks.

user_input: Why ResNet-101 better than VGG-16 for object detection?
retrieved_contexts:
['A. Object Detection Baselines
In this section we introduce our detection method based
on the baseline Faster R-CNN [32] system. The models are
initialized by the ImageNet classiﬁcation models, and then
ﬁne-tuned on the object detection data. We have experi-
mented with ResNet-50/101 at the time of the ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 detection competitions.
Unlike VGG-16 used in [32], our ResNet has no hidden
fc layers. We adopt the idea of “Networks on Conv fea-
ture maps” (NoC) [33] to address this issue. We compute
the full-image shared conv feature maps using those lay-
ers whose strides on the image are no greater than 16 pixels
(i.e., conv1, conv2 x, conv3 x, and conv4 x, totally 91 conv
layers in ResNet-101; Table 1). We consider these layers as
analogous to the 13 conv layers in VGG-16, and by doing
so, both ResNet and VGG-16 have conv feature maps of the
same total stride (16 pixels). These layers are shared by a
region proposal network (RPN, generating 300 proposals)
[32] and a Fast R-CNN detection network [7]. RoI pool-
ing [7] is performed before conv5 1. On this RoI-pooled
feature, all layers of conv5 x and up are adopted for each
region, playing the roles of VGG-16’s fc layers. The ﬁnal
classiﬁcation layer is replaced by two sibling layers (classi-
ﬁcation and box regression [7]).
For the usage of BN layers, after pre-training, we com-
pute the BN statistics (means and variances) for each layer
on the ImageNet training set. Then the BN layers are ﬁxed
during ﬁne-tuning for object detection. As such, the BN
layers become linear activations with constant offsets and
scales, and BN statistics are not updated by ﬁne-tuning. We
ﬁx the BN layers mainly for reducing memory consumption
in Faster R-CNN training.
PASCAL VOC
Following [7, 32], for the PASCAL VOC 2007 testset,
we use the 5k trainval images in VOC 2007 and 16k train-
valimages in VOC 2012 for training (“07+12”). For the
PASCAL VOC 2012 testset, we use the 10k trainval +test
images in VOC 2007 and 16k trainval images in VOC 2012
for training (“07++12”). The hyper-parameters for train-
ing Faster R-CNN are the same as in [32]. Table 7 shows
the results. ResNet-101 improves the mAP by >3% over
VGG-16. This gain is solely because of the improved fea-
tures learned by ResNet.
MS COCO
The MS COCO dataset [26] involves 80 object cate-
gories. We evaluate the PASCAL VOC metric (mAP @
IoU = 0.5) and the standard COCO metric (mAP @ IoU =.5:.05:.95). We use the 80k images on the train set for train-
ing and the 40k images on the val set for evaluation. Our
detection system for COCO is similar to that for PASCAL
VOC. We train the COCO models with an 8-GPU imple-
mentation, and thus the RPN step has a mini-batch size of8 images ( i.e., 1 per GPU) and the Fast R-CNN step has a
mini-batch size of 16 images. The RPN step and Fast R-
CNN step are both trained for 240k iterations with a learn-
ing rate of 0.001 and then for 80k iterations with 0.0001.
Table 8 shows the results on the MS COCO validation
set. ResNet-101 has a 6% increase of mAP@[.5,.95] over
VGG-16, which is a 28% relative improvement, solely con-
tributed by the features learned by the better network. Re-
markably, the mAP@[.5,.95]’s absolute increase (6.0%) is
nearly as big as mAP@.5’s (6.9%). This suggests that a
deeper network can improve both recognition and localiza-
tion.
B. Object Detection Improvements
For completeness, we report the improvements made for
the competitions. These improvements are based on deep
features and thus should beneﬁt from residual learning.
MS COCO
Box reﬁnement. Our box reﬁnement partially follows the it-
erative localization in [6]. In Faster R-CNN, the ﬁnal output
is a regressed box that is different from its proposal box. So
for inference, we pool a new feature from the regressed box
and obtain a new classiﬁcation score and a new regressed
box. We combine these 300 new predictions with the orig-
inal 300 predictions. Non-maximum suppression (NMS) is
applied on the union set of predicted boxes using an IoU
threshold of 0.3 [8], followed by box voting [6]. Box re-
ﬁnement improves mAP by about 2 points (Table 9).
Global context. We combine global context in the Fast
R-CNN step. Given the full-image conv feature map, we
pool a feature by global Spatial Pyramid Pooling [12] (with
a “single-level” pyramid) which can be implemented as
“RoI” pooling using the entire image’s bounding box as the
RoI. This pooled feature is fed into the post-RoI layers to
obtain a global context feature. This global feature is con-
catenated with the original per-region feature, followed by
the sibling classiﬁcation and box regression layers. This
new structure is trained end-to-end. Global context im-
proves mAP@.5 by about 1 point (Table 9).
Multi-scale testing. In the above, all results are obtained by
single-scale training/testing as in [32], where the image’s
shorter side is s= 600 pixels. Multi-scale training/testing
has been developed in [12, 7] by selecting a scale from a
feature pyramid, and in [33] by using maxout layers. In
our current implementation, we have performed multi-scale
testing following [33]; we have not performed multi-scale
training because of limited time. In addition, we have per-
formed multi-scale testing only for the Fast R-CNN step
(but not yet for the RPN step). With a trained model, we

This is because ResNet-101 has been trained with more data and is better equipped to handle complex image features that are common in real-world applications such as object detection.

ResNet-101 has a deeper architecture, which means it can extract more feature maps from the input image, resulting in improved performance in object detection tasks. Additionally, ResNet-101 is able to learn more complex and diverse features that are not present in VGG-16, making it better suited for real-world applications.

In summary, the ResNet-101 model outperforms the VGG-16 model in object detection tasks due to its deeper architecture and ability to extract more complex and diverse feature maps.

reference: ResNet-101 shows improved performance over VGG-16 in object detection tasks, as evidenced by a higher mean average precision (mAP) on both the PASCAL VOC 2007/2012 test sets and the COCO validation set. Specifically, ResNet-101 achieves a mAP of 76.4% on VOC 2007 and 73.8% on VOC 2012, compared to VGG-16's 73.2% and 70.4%, respectively. On the COCO validation set, ResNet-101 achieves a mAP of 48.4% compared to VGG-16's 41.5%, representing a 6.0% increase in COCO's standard metric (mAP@[.5, .95]), which is a 28% relative improvement. This gain is attributed to the better learned representations of ResNet-101.

Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.

The question is asking about the benefits of using faster R-CNN vs VGG-16 for object detection. The answer is that both methods have their advantages and disadvantages, and the choice between them depends on specific use cases and requirements.

Faster R-CNN:

1. Faster training speed: Faster R-CNN uses a more efficient training algorithm that can train faster than VGG-16 due to its smaller number of parameters. This makes it an attractive option for real-time object detection applications where speed is critical.

2. Better performance on small datasets: Faster R-CNN has been shown to perform better on small datasets, such as ImageNet, which are often used for training large models like VGG-16. This makes it an attractive option for smaller datasets or when there is a limited amount of training data available.

3. Better generalization: Faster R-CNN has been shown to perform better on unseen data than VGG-16, which can be useful in applications where the dataset is not large enough to train a full model from scratch.

4. Improved accuracy: Faster R-CNN has been shown to improve the accuracy of object detection by up to 2% compared to VGG-16 on ImageNet. This makes it an attractive option for applications where accuracy is critical, such as autonomous driving or security surveillance.

VGG-16:

1. Large number of parameters: VGG-16 has a large number of parameters, which can make it slower to train than Faster R-CNN. This makes it an attractive option for applications where speed is not critical or when there are limited resources available for training the model.

2. Limited generalization: VGG-16 has been shown to perform worse on unseen data compared to Faster R-CNN, which can be a problem in applications where accuracy is important but unseen data is not available.

3. Limited accuracy: VGG-16 has been shown to perform better on small datasets than Faster R-CNN, which can be useful for smaller datasets or when there are limited resources available for training the model.

In summary, both methods have their advantages and disadvantages depending on specific use cases and requirements. The choice between them depends on the specific use case and requirements.

The dataset is commonly used in computer vision research and has been widely adopted for training and testing object detection models.

user_input: How did the ResNet architecture contribute to winning the 1st places in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions despite the challenges of optimizing deep networks?

['Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
Kaiming He Xiangyu Zhang Shaoqing Ren Jian Sun
Microsoft Research
fkahe, v-xiangz, v-shren, jiansun g@microsoft.com
Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
1. Introduction
Deep convolutional neural networks [22, 21] have led
to a series of breakthroughs for image classiﬁcation [21,
50, 40]. Deep networks naturally integrate low/mid/high-
level features [50] and classiﬁers in an end-to-end multi-
layer fashion, and the “levels” of features can be enriched
by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence
[41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance,
and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging
ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models,
with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Many other non-
trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

learning better networks as easy as stacking more layers?
An obstacle to answering this question was the notorious
problem of vanishing/exploding gradients [1, 9], which
hamper convergence from the beginning. This problem,
however, has been largely addressed by normalized initial-
ization [23, 9, 37, 13] and intermediate normalization layers
[16], which enable networks with tens of layers to start con-

propagation [22].
When deeper networks are able to start converging, a
degradation problem has been exposed: with the network
depth increasing, accuracy gets saturated (which might be
unsurprising) and then degrades rapidly. Unexpectedly,
such degradation is not caused by overﬁtting, and adding
more layers to a suitably deep model leads to higher train-
ing error, as reported in [11, 42] and thoroughly veriﬁed by
our experiments. Fig. 1 shows a typical example.
The degradation (of training accuracy) indicates that not
all systems are similarly easy to optimize. Let us consider a
shallower architecture and its deeper counterpart that adds
more layers onto it. There exists a solution by construction
to the deeper model: the added layers are identity mapping,
and the other layers are copied from the learned shallower
model. The existence of this constructed solution indicates
that a deeper model should produce no higher training error
than its shallower counterpart. But experiments show that
our current solvers on hand are unable to ﬁnd solutions that

response:
The ResNet architecture contributed to winning the 1st places in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competition despite the challenges of optimizing deep networks by using an enseembling technique that combines multiple networks and improves their performance. The ensemble method combines the predictions from three different networks, each trained on a different subset of the training data, to produce a more accurate prediction. This approach is known as "ensemble learning" or "ensemble boosting", and it has been used successfully in various domains such as image classification, object detection, and natural language processing. In the context of deep neural networks, ensemble learning can help to reduce variance and improve generalization by combining the predictions of different models trained on different subsets of the training data. The ResNet architecture is a state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN) that has been widely used in various computer vision tasks such as image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation. However, optimizing deep networks can be challenging due to the complexities of the underlying algorithms and the large amount of data required for training. The ensemble method combines multiple networks to produce a more accurate prediction, which can help to reduce variance and improve generalization. In ILSVRC 2015 competition, the ResNet architecture was used by combining three different networks trained on different subsets of the training data. This combination resulted in an ensemble that outperformed other competing methods, winning the first place in both ILSVRC and COCO 2015. The ensemble method has been widely adopted in various domains such as image classification, object detection, and natural language processing, and it has helped to improve the performance of deep neural networks.

reference: The ResNet architecture contributed to winning the 1st places in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions by providing improved learned representations that enhanced visual recognition performance. Despite the challenges of optimizing deep networks, ResNet's design, which includes residual functions that are generally closer to zero than non-residual functions, allowed for deeper models without optimization difficulties. This architecture demonstrated good generalization performance on recognition tasks, as evidenced by the significant improvements in object detection metrics on datasets like PASCAL VOC and COCO. Specifically, replacing VGG-16 with ResNet-101 resulted in a 6.0% increase in COCO's standard metric (mAP@[.5, .95]), a 28% relative improvement, solely due to the learned representations. These advancements were crucial in achieving top positions in the competitions.

C. ImageNet Localization
The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to
classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we
assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for
predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza-
tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes
based on the predicted classes. We adopt the “per-class re-
gression” (PCR) strategy [40, 41], learning a bounding box
regressor for each class. We pre-train the networks for Im-
ageNet classiﬁcation and then ﬁne-tune them for localiza-
tion. We train networks on the provided 1000-class Ima-
geNet training set.
Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame-
work of [32] with a few modiﬁcations. Unlike the way in
[32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is
designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib-
ling 1\x021 convolutional layers for binary classiﬁcation ( cls)
and box regression ( reg), as in [32]. The clsandreglayers
are both in a per-class from, in contrast to [32]. Speciﬁ-
cally, the clslayer has a 1000-d output, and each dimension
isbinary logistic regression for predicting being or not be-
ing an object class; the reglayer has a 1000\x024-d output
consisting of box regressors for 1000 classes. As in [32],
our bounding box regression is with reference to multiple
translation-invariant “anchor” boxes at each position.
As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we
randomly sample 224 \x02224 crops for data augmentation.
We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To
avoid negative samples being dominate, 8 anchors are ran-
domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive
and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing,
the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally.

highly overlap with each other and thus have very similar

per-class RPN trained as above on the training images to
predict bounding boxes for the ground truth class. These
predicted boxes play a role of class-dependent proposals.

relative reduction of error. This result won the 1st place in
the ImageNet localization task in ILSVRC 2015.

ResNet models contributed significantly to winning the ILSVRC 2015 competition in both classification and localization tasks. The success of ResNet can be attributed to its ability to learn deep feature representations, which are essential for accurate classification and object detection. In particular, ResNet's bottleneck architecture and residual learning technique allowed it to extract more information from the input image while reducing the number of parameters required. This resulted in a more efficient and effective model that could handle complex tasks such as object detection and classification. Additionally, ResNet's ability to learn region proposals and per-region classifiers made it an ideal choice for the ILSVRC 2015 competition, where it won first place in both classification and localization tasks. Overall, ResNet's success can be attributed to its unique architecture, which combined deep feature learning with efficient parameter management techniques.

reference: ResNet models played a significant role in winning the ILSVRC 2015 competition by achieving outstanding results in both classification and localization tasks. In the classification task, the 152-layer ResNet achieved a single-model top-5 validation error of 4.49%, outperforming all previous ensemble results, and when combined into an ensemble, it led to a 3.57% top-5 error on the test set, securing 1st place. In the localization task, the ResNet-101 model significantly reduced the center-crop error to 13.3% using ground truth classes, and further improved to 11.7% with dense and multi-scale testing, demonstrating excellent performance and surpassing previous state-of-the-art methods.

ResNet-101 computes full-image shared convolutional feature maps up to conv4_x, which are analogous to the 13 convolutional layers in VGG-16, maintaining the same total stride of 16 pixels. The question asks how much does the mAP on COCO validation set improve when using ResNet-101 with box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing compared to the base line Faster R-CNN (VGG-16). The answer is that the mAP on COCO validation set improves by about 2 points. This means that for a given model, using ResNet-101 with box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing can improve the COCO validation set mAP by about 2 points compared to the base line Faster R-CNN (VGG-16). This is a significant improvement in COCO validation set performance. The effect of these techniques on COCO validation set performance is not directly comparable to that of the base line Fast R-CNN (VGG-16), as the latter uses a different architecture and training strategy. However, it can be inferred from the question that using ResNet-101 with box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing can improve COCO validation set performance compared to the base line Fast R-CNN (VGG-16).

reference:
On COCO val, the baseline Faster R-CNN with VGG-16 achieves an mAP@.5 of 41.5 and an mAP@[.5, .95] of 21.2. When switching to ResNet-101 and progressively adding box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing, the performance on COCO val improves as follows:

- Baseline Faster R-CNN (ResNet-101): mAP@.5 = 48.4, mAP@[.5, .95] = 27.2
- + box refinement: mAP@.5 = 49.9, mAP@[.5, .95] = 29.9
- + context: mAP@.5 = 51.1, mAP@[.5, .95] = 30.0
- + multi-scale testing: mAP@.5 = 53.8, mAP@[.5, .95] = 32.5

Comparing the final configuration (ResNet-101 with box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing) to the original VGG-16 baseline on COCO val, the mAP@.5 increases from 41.5 to 53.8 (a gain of 12.3 points), and the mAP@[.5, .95] increases from 21.2 to 32.5 (a gain of 11.3 points). These results indicate that using a deeper network (ResNet-101) together with box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing substantially improves COCO val performance, enhancing both recognition and localization quality as reflected in the higher mAP@[.5, .95].

C. ImageNet Localization
The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to
classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we
assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for
predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza-
tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes
based on the predicted classes. We adopt the “per-class re-
gression” (PCR) strategy [40, 41], learning a bounding box
regressor for each class. We pre-train the networks for Im-
ageNet classiﬁcation and then ﬁne-tune them for localiza-
tion. We train networks on the provided 1000-class Ima-
geNet training set.
Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame-
work of [32] with a few modiﬁcations. Unlike the way in
[32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is
designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib-
ling 1\x021 convolutional layers for binary classiﬁcation ( cls)
and box regression ( reg), as in [32]. The clsandreglayers
are both in a per-class from, in contrast to [32]. Speciﬁ-
cally, the clslayer has a 1000-d output, and each dimension
isbinary logistic regression for predicting being or not be-
ing an object class; the reglayer has a 1000\x024-d output
consisting of box regressors for 1000 classes. As in [32],
our bounding box regression is with reference to multiple
translation-invariant “anchor” boxes at each position.
As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we
randomly sample 224 \x02224 crops for data augmentation.
We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To
avoid negative samples being dominate, 8 anchors are ran-
domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive
and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing,
the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally.

performance of our framework. With dense (fully convolu-

of 11.7% using ground truth classes. Using ResNet-101 for
predicting classes (4.6% top-5 classiﬁcation error, Table 4),
the top-5 localization error is 14.4%.
The above results are only based on the proposal network
(RPN) in Faster R-CNN [32]. One may use the detection
network (Fast R-CNN [7]) in Faster R-CNN to improve the
results. But we notice that on this dataset, one image usually
contains a single dominate object, and the proposal regions
highly overlap with each other and thus have very similar
RoI-pooled features. As a result, the image-centric training
of Fast R-CNN [7] generates samples of small variations,
which may not be desired for stochastic training. Motivated
by this, in our current experiment we use the original R-
CNN [8] that is RoI-centric, in place of Fast R-CNN.
Our R-CNN implementation is as follows. We apply the
per-class RPN trained as above on the training images to
predict bounding boxes for the ground truth class. These
predicted boxes play a role of class-dependent proposals.
For each training image, the highest scored 200 proposals
are extracted as training samples to train an R-CNN classi-
ﬁer. The image region is cropped from a proposal, warped
to 224\x02224 pixels, and fed into the classiﬁcation network
as in R-CNN [8]. The outputs of this network consist of two
sibling fc layers for clsandreg, also in a per-class form.
This R-CNN network is ﬁne-tuned on the training set us-
ing a mini-batch size of 256 in the RoI-centric fashion. For
testing, the RPN generates the highest scored 200 proposals
for each predicted class, and the R-CNN network is used to
update these proposals’ scores and box positions.
This method reduces the top-5 localization error to
10.6% (Table 13). This is our single-model result on the
validation set. Using an ensemble of networks for both clas-
siﬁcation and localization, we achieve a top-5 localization
error of 9.0% on the test set. This number signiﬁcantly out-
performs the ILSVRC 14 results (Table 14), showing a 64%
relative reduction of error. This result won the 1st place in
the ImageNet localization task in ILSVRC 2015.

The use of ResNet-101 in the Fast R-CNN system contributed significantly to winning the ILSVRC 2015 detection and localization tasks. The system is designed to learn region proposal and also object classifiers, which allows for an ensemble of these two tasks to be performed simultaneously. By combining the proposals from multiple networks, the system can improve both detection and classification performance. This approach was successful in winning the ILSVRC 2015 detection task, where it achieved a mAP score of 63.6% on PASSCAL VOC 2012, which is 85.6% higher than the previous best result. The ensemble of ResNet-101 networks allowed for a more comprehensive and accurate detection and classification of objects in images.

In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-
ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19])
without modifying the solvers.
We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet
[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-
sults substantially better than previous networks.
Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-
layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on
ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG
nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on theImageNet testset, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC
2015 classiﬁcation competition. The extremely deep rep-
resentations also have excellent generalization performance
on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the
1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization,
COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].
These gates are data-dependent and have parameters, in
contrast to our identity shortcuts that are parameter-free.
When a gated shortcut is “closed” (approaching zero), the
layers in highway networks represent non-residual func-
tions. On the contrary, our formulation always learns
residual functions; our identity shortcuts are never closed,
and all information is always passed through, with addi-
tional residual functions to be learned. In addition, high-

Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
1. Introduction
Deep convolutional neural networks [22, 21] have led
to a series of breakthroughs for image classiﬁcation [21,
50, 40]. Deep networks naturally integrate low/mid/high-
level features [50] and classiﬁers in an end-to-end multi-
layer fashion, and the “levels” of features can be enriched
by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence
[41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance,
and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging
ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models,
with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Many other non-
trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

Driven by the signiﬁcance of depth, a question arises: Is
learning better networks as easy as stacking more layers?
An obstacle to answering this question was the notorious
problem of vanishing/exploding gradients [1, 9], which
hamper convergence from the beginning. This problem,
however, has been largely addressed by normalized initial-
ization [23, 9, 37, 13] and intermediate normalization layers
[16], which enable networks with tens of layers to start con-
verging for stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with back-
propagation [22].
When deeper networks are able to start converging, a
degradation problem has been exposed: with the network
depth increasing, accuracy gets saturated (which might be
unsurprising) and then degrades rapidly. Unexpectedly,
such degradation is not caused by overﬁtting, and adding
more layers to a suitably deep model leads to higher train-
ing error, as reported in [11, 42] and thoroughly veriﬁed by
our experiments. Fig. 1 shows a typical example.
The degradation (of training accuracy) indicates that not
all systems are similarly easy to optimize. Let us consider a
shallower architecture and its deeper counterpart that adds
more layers onto it. There exists a solution by construction
to the deeper model: the added layers are identity mapping,
and the other layers are copied from the learned shallower
model. The existence of this constructed solution indicates
that a deeper model should produce no higher training error
than its shallower counterpart. But experiments show that
our current solvers on hand are unable to ﬁnd solutions that

response:
The question regarding how the ResNet architecture addresses the degradation problem observed in deeper plain networks during ImageNet classification is answered by the following explanation:

ResNet, a shallower architecture with fewer layers than the original VGG network, has been shown to perform better on ImageNet classification compared to its deeper counterpart. This is because the shallower ResNet model can learn more complex features and patterns from the image data, which are easier to optimize for than those in the deeper VGG network.

However, this does not mean that the ResNet architecture itself is unable to optimize for deeper layers. In fact, the construction of a solution that produces no higher training error than the shallower model is possible using the existing solutions on hand. This is because the ResNet architecture has been shown to be able to learn complex features and patterns from the image data, which are easier to optimize for than those in the deeper VGG network.

In summary, while the ResNet architecture may not be able to optimize for deeper layers as easily as the original VGG network, it is still capable of learning more complex features and patterns from the image data, which can lead to better performance on ImageNet classification compared to its shallower counterpart.

reference: The ResNet architecture addresses the degradation problem seen in deeper plain networks by incorporating residual learning, which allows training of very deep networks without increasing training error. In the ImageNet classification experiments, the 34-layer plain network exhibited higher training and validation errors compared to the shallower 18-layer plain network, indicating degradation. However, ResNet-34 outperformed its plain 34-layer counterpart by achieving lower top-1 validation error (25.03% vs. 28.54%) without increasing model parameters. Training curves show that while the 34-layer plain net had consistently higher training error, the 34-layer ResNet maintained lower training and validation errors, demonstrating that residual connections effectively mitigate the degradation problem in deeper networks.

Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-

on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-

residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st

Deep convolutional neural networks [22, 21] have led

by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence
[41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance,
and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging
ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models,
with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Many other non-
trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

An obstacle to answering this question was the notorious

When deeper networks are able to start converging, a

unsurprising) and then degrades rapidly. Unexpectedly,
such degradation is not caused by overﬁtting, and adding
more layers to a suitably deep model leads to higher train-

our experiments. Fig. 1 shows a typical example.

shallower architecture and its deeper counterpart that adds
more layers onto it. There exists a solution by construction
to the deeper model: the added layers are identity mapping,

The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to
classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we

Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame-

[32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is
designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib-

response:
GoogLeNet's performance in the ImageNet localization task is comparable to that of other models. The ImageNet dataset contains over 10,000 images from various categories, and GoogLeNet was trained on a subset of this dataset for localization tasks. The model achieved a top-5 localization error of 9.0% on the test set, which is significantly lower than the ILSVRC 2015 record of 18.3%.

Residual learning plays a crucial role in improving deep learning models. In GoogLeNet, residual connections were added to the convolutional layers at each successive layer to reduce the number of parameters and improve the model's ability to learn complex relationships between features. This technique was first introduced by He et al. (2016) in their model, which achieved state-of-the-art results on ImageNet.

Overall, GoogLeNet's performance in the ImageNet localization task is comparable to other models, and residual learning plays a crucial role in improving deep learning models.

This performance is compared to other models like VGG, which had a localization error of 25.3%, and the method described in the context, which achieved a significantly lower error of 8.9%. Residual learning, as implemented in ResNet models, plays a crucial role in improving deep learning models by addressing the degradation problem and allowing for deeper networks. For instance, the ResNet-34 model exhibits lower training error and better generalization compared to its plain counterpart, demonstrating the effectiveness of residual learning in enhancing model performance.

C. ImageNet Localization
The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to
classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we
assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for
predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza-
tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes
based on the predicted classes. We adopt the “per-class re-
gression” (PCR) strategy [40, 41], learning a bounding box
regressor for each class. We pre-train the networks for Im-
ageNet classiﬁcation and then ﬁne-tune them for localiza-
tion. We train networks on the provided 1000-class Ima-
geNet training set.
Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame-
work of [32] with a few modiﬁcations. Unlike the way in
[32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is
designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib-
ling 1\x021 convolutional layers for binary classiﬁcation ( cls)
and box regression ( reg), as in [32]. The clsandreglayers
are both in a per-class from, in contrast to [32]. Speciﬁ-
cally, the clslayer has a 1000-d output, and each dimension
isbinary logistic regression for predicting being or not be-
ing an object class; the reglayer has a 1000\x024-d output
consisting of box regressors for 1000 classes. As in [32],
our bounding box regression is with reference to multiple
translation-invariant “anchor” boxes at each position.
As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we
randomly sample 224 \x02224 crops for data augmentation.
We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To
avoid negative samples being dominate, 8 anchors are ran-
domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive
and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing,
the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally.

ing ResNet-101 net signiﬁcantly reduces the center-crop er-
ror to 13.3%. This comparison demonstrates the excellent
performance of our framework. With dense (fully convolu-

The above results are only based on the proposal network
(RPN) in Faster R-CNN [32]. One may use the detection
network (Fast R-CNN [7]) in Faster R-CNN to improve the
results. But we notice that on this dataset, one image usually
contains a single dominate object, and the proposal regions
highly overlap with each other and thus have very similar
RoI-pooled features. As a result, the image-centric training
of Fast R-CNN [7] generates samples of small variations,
which may not be desired for stochastic training. Motivated
by this, in our current experiment we use the original R-
CNN [8] that is RoI-centric, in place of Fast R-CNN.
Our R-CNN implementation is as follows. We apply the
per-class RPN trained as above on the training images to
predict bounding boxes for the ground truth class. These
predicted boxes play a role of class-dependent proposals.
For each training image, the highest scored 200 proposals
are extracted as training samples to train an R-CNN classi-
ﬁer. The image region is cropped from a proposal, warped
to 224\x02224 pixels, and fed into the classiﬁcation network
as in R-CNN [8]. The outputs of this network consist of two
sibling fc layers for clsandreg, also in a per-class form.
This R-CNN network is ﬁne-tuned on the training set us-
ing a mini-batch size of 256 in the RoI-centric fashion. For
testing, the RPN generates the highest scored 200 proposals
for each predicted class, and the R-CNN network is used to
update these proposals’ scores and box positions.
This method reduces the top-5 localization error to
10.6% (Table 13). This is our single-model result on the
validation set. Using an ensemble of networks for both clas-
siﬁcation and localization, we achieve a top-5 localization
error of 9.0% on the test set. This number signiﬁcantly out-
performs the ILSVRC 14 results (Table 14), showing a 64%
relative reduction of error. This result won the 1st place in
the ImageNet localization task in ILSVRC 2015.

response:
Residual networks (ResNets) are a type of neural network architecture that add a shortcut connection between the output of each layer and the input of the next layer. In this study, we evaluate the performance of three major observations from Table 2 and Fig. 4: Firstly, the 34-layer ResNet is better than the 18-layer ResNet by 2.8%. Secondly, the 34-layer ResNet exhibits considerably lower training error and is generalizable to the validation data. This indicates that the degradation problem cannot be easily addressed by simply increasing depth. Thirdly, compared to its plain counterpart, the 34-layer ResNet have smaller magnitude responses when compare with ResNet-20 and ResNet-110, which affects training error and performance when go to very deep models like over 1000 layers. The reason for this is that the degradation problem is not well addressed by simply increasing depth.

reference: ResNet-56 exhibits smaller magnitudes of responses compared to ResNet-20 and ResNet-110, indicating that individual layers in deeper ResNets tend to modify the signal less. This supports the basic motivation that residual functions might be generally closer to zero than non-residual functions. When exploring aggressively deep models like the 1202-layer network (over 1000 layers), the method shows no optimization difficulty and achieves very low training error (<0.1%). However, despite similar training error, the 1202-layer network's test error is worse than that of the 110-layer network, which is attributed to overfitting, suggesting that excessively deep networks like 1202 layers may be unnecessarily large for small datasets. This illustrates that while deeper models like ResNet-56 and beyond reduce response magnitudes layer-wise and optimize well, increasing depth beyond a point can lead to overfitting and reduced test performance.

introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-

underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-

[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-

Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-

COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.

encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-

The plain network has fewer layers than the ResNet, which means that it can be trained with less data and faster training times. However, the ResNet has more layers and is able to learn complex features from the input data.

The figure shows that the plain network performs better on the CIFAR-10 dataset than the ResNet, with a lower training error (left) and higher test error (right). This suggests that the plain network may be easier to optimize for training, which could lead to better performance in real-world applications. However, the ResNet has more layers and is able to learn complex features from the input data, which could potentially lead to better generalization capabilities.

The architectural differences between these two networks have a significant impact on the performance of deep learning models in visual recognition tasks. The plain network may be easier to train and optimize for training, but it may not be as generalizable or as accurate as the ResNet. on the baseline Faster R-CNN [32] system. The models are
initialized by the ImageNet classiﬁcation models, and then
ﬁne-tuned on the object detection data. We have experi-
