The VGG-19 model is a complex and architecturally significant model that has been widely used in various computer vision applications. It consists of 19 convolutional layers, each with a kernel size of 3x3, followed by max pooling and a fully connected layer with 512 neurons. The VGG-19 model is based on the VGG architecture, which is a popular and widely used set of convolutional layers for image classification and object detection tasks.

In terms of complexity, the VGG-19 model is significantly more complex than the 34-layer plain and residual networks that have been used in visual recognition tasks. The 34-layer network consists of 34 convolutional layers with a kernel size of 3x3 each, followed by max pooling and a fully connected layer with 1024 neurons. This architecture is known for its ability to capture complex features and perform well on various visual recognition tasks.

In terms of architectural complexity, the VGG-19 model is also more complex than the plain and residual networks that have been used in visual recognition tasks. The plain network consists of a single convolutional layer with a kernel size of 3x3 followed by max pooling, while the VGG-19 model has multiple convolutional layers with different kernel sizes. This architecture is known for its ability to capture complex features and perform well on various visual recognition tasks.

Overall, the VGG-19 model is a highly complex and architecturally significant model that has been widely used in various computer vision applications. Its complexity and architecture are known for their ability to capture complex features and perform well on various visual recognition tasks.

reference: The VGG-19 model, used as a reference, has a complexity of 19.6 billion FLOPs, which is significantly higher than the 34-layer plain and residual networks, both having 3.6 billion FLOPs. The VGG-19 architecture consists of convolutional layers with mostly 3x3 filters, following design rules where layers with the same output feature map size have the same number of filters, and the number of filters doubles when the feature map size is halved. It ends with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way fully-connected layer with softmax. In contrast, the 34-layer plain network has fewer filters and lower complexity, while the residual network introduces shortcut connections to the plain network, allowing identity mapping or projection shortcuts to match dimensions. Both the plain and residual networks are optimized for efficiency, maintaining lower complexity compared to VGG-19 while still being effective for visual recognition tasks.

The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to
classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we

The answer is that GoogLeNet achieves better top-5 error rates on ImageNet classification compared to deep plain networks because it uses a deeper network architecture with more layers and more parameters. This allows the network to learn more complex features and make more accurate predictions, which leads to better performance in image recognition tasks.

ResNet-101 is an updated version of the popular ResNet architecture that has been shown to perform well on various image recognition tasks, including classification and detection. The detection performance improvements using ResNet-101 on datasets like PASSAL VOC and ImageNet are related to these classification advances.

In summary, GoogLeNet's deeper network architecture with more layers and parameters leads to better top-5 error rates on ImageNet classification compared to deep plain networks, while ResNet-101's updated version has been shown to perform well in various image recognition tasks.

reference: GoogLeNet achieves better top-5 error rates on ImageNet classification partly because it overcomes the optimization difficulties faced by deep plain networks, which have exponentially low convergence rates affecting training error reduction. Unlike plain networks, residual networks (ResNets) introduce shortcut connections that help address the degradation problem and allow for accuracy gains from increased depth, as seen in the improved error rates of ResNet-34 and deeper variants compared to plain nets. Moreover, these classification improvements translate into enhanced detection performance when using ResNet-101 as the backbone for object detection systems like Faster R-CNN. Specifically, ResNet-101 based detectors achieve state-of-the-art mAP scores on PASCAL VOC 2007 (85.6%), PASCAL VOC 2012 (83.8%), and the ImageNet Detection task, significantly outperforming previous benchmarks and showing how classification advances with residual learning benefit detection tasks as well.

In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-
ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19])
without modifying the solvers.
We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet
[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-
sults substantially better than previous networks.
Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-
layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on
ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG
nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on theImageNet testset, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC
2015 classiﬁcation competition. The extremely deep rep-
resentations also have excellent generalization performance
on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the
1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization,
COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].
These gates are data-dependent and have parameters, in
contrast to our identity shortcuts that are parameter-free.
When a gated shortcut is “closed” (approaching zero), the
layers in highway networks represent non-residual func-
tions. On the contrary, our formulation always learns
residual functions; our identity shortcuts are never closed,
and all information is always passed through, with addi-
tional residual functions to be learned. In addition, high-

Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
1. Introduction
Deep convolutional neural networks [22, 21] have led
to a series of breakthroughs for image classiﬁcation [21,
50, 40]. Deep networks naturally integrate low/mid/high-
level features [50] and classiﬁers in an end-to-end multi-
layer fashion, and the “levels” of features can be enriched
by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence
[41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance,
and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging
ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models,
with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Many other non-
trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

Driven by the signiﬁcance of depth, a question arises: Is
learning better networks as easy as stacking more layers?
An obstacle to answering this question was the notorious
problem of vanishing/exploding gradients [1, 9], which
hamper convergence from the beginning. This problem,
however, has been largely addressed by normalized initial-
ization [23, 9, 37, 13] and intermediate normalization layers
[16], which enable networks with tens of layers to start con-

When deeper networks are able to start converging, a
degradation problem has been exposed: with the network
depth increasing, accuracy gets saturated (which might be
unsurprising) and then degrades rapidly. Unexpectedly,
such degradation is not caused by overﬁtting, and adding
more layers to a suitably deep model leads to higher train-
ing error, as reported in [11, 42] and thoroughly veriﬁed by
our experiments. Fig. 1 shows a typical example.
The degradation (of training accuracy) indicates that not
all systems are similarly easy to optimize. Let us consider a
shallower architecture and its deeper counterpart that adds
more layers onto it. There exists a solution by construction
to the deeper model: the added layers are identity mapping,
and the other layers are copied from the learned shallower
model. The existence of this constructed solution indicates
that a deeper model should produce no higher training error
than its shallower counterpart. But experiments show that
our current solvers on hand are unable to ﬁnd solutions that

response:
Researchers have explored the possibility of using very deep networks (VGG-16, ResNet-101) for object detection on popular datasets such as PASCAL VOC 2007 and COCO. The reason for this is that these networks are known to perform well in image recognition tasks, and their ability to extract feature maps from deep layers has been shown to improve the performance of object detection.

Researchers have also found that using deeper networks (34-layer and 152-layer ResNet) can lead to better results than simpler networks such as VGG-16. This is because deeper networks have more parameters, which means they can capture more information from the input image. Additionally, deeper networks tend to have better generalization ability, meaning that they can perform well on new datasets even if they are not trained on them.

In terms of accuracy and top-5 error, the 1st place in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competition was won by a ResNet-101 model. This is because ResNets have been shown to perform well in image recognition tasks, and their ability to extract feature maps from deep layers has been shown to improve the performance of object detection. The 34-layer ResNet also performed well, but it was not able to achieve the same level of accuracy as the 101-layer ResNet.

Overall, the use of deeper networks (34-layer and 152-layer ResNets) for object detection on PASCAL VOC 2007 and COCO datasets has led to better results than simpler networks such as VGG-16. The ability to capture more information from the input image, along with deeper networks' ability to perform well in new datasets, make these models a strong contender for object detection on popular datasets.

reference: ResNets are better than plain nets for very deep networks because they effectively address the degradation problem that plain nets face during optimization. Specifically, residual learning allows the 34-layer ResNet to achieve considerably lower training error and better generalize to validation data compared to its plain counterpart, as shown by a 2.8% improvement in accuracy. Additionally, residual networks converge faster and show accuracy gains with increased depth, unlike plain networks that may have exponentially low convergence rates. Regarding comparison with state-of-the-art methods, the 152-layer ResNet achieves a single-model top-5 validation error of 4.49%, which outperforms all previous ensemble results reported before. An ensemble combining six models including two 152-layer ResNets further reduces the top-5 error on the ImageNet test set to 3.57%, winning first place in the ILSVRC 2015 competition. These performance metrics demonstrate that deeper ResNets significantly improve accuracy and benchmark results compared to plain networks and other leading architectures.

difﬁculty is a fundamental problem.
Fig. 6 (middle) shows the behaviors of ResNets. Also
similar to the ImageNet cases (Fig. 4, right), our ResNets
manage to overcome the optimization difﬁculty and demon-
strate accuracy gains when the depth increases.
We further explore n= 18 that leads to a 110-layer
ResNet. In this case, we ﬁnd that the initial learning rate
of 0.1 is slightly too large to start converging5. So we use
0.01 to warm up the training until the training error is below
80% (about 400 iterations), and then go back to 0.1 and con-
tinue training. The rest of the learning schedule is as done
previously. This 110-layer network converges well (Fig. 6,
middle). It has fewer parameters than other deep and thin
5With an initial learning rate of 0.1, it starts converging ( <90% error)
after several epochs, but still reaches similar accuracy.

are comparably good or better than the constructed solution
(or unable to do so in feasible time).
In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-
ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19])
without modifying the solvers.
We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet
[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-
sults substantially better than previous networks.
Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-
layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on
ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG
nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on theImageNet testset, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC
2015 classiﬁcation competition. The extremely deep rep-
resentations also have excellent generalization performance
on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the
1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization,
COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].
These gates are data-dependent and have parameters, in
contrast to our identity shortcuts that are parameter-free.
When a gated shortcut is “closed” (approaching zero), the
layers in highway networks represent non-residual func-
tions. On the contrary, our formulation always learns
residual functions; our identity shortcuts are never closed,
and all information is always passed through, with addi-
tional residual functions to be learned. In addition, high-
2', 'way networks have not demonstrated accuracy gains with
extremely increased depth ( e.g., over 100 layers).
3. Deep Residual Learning
3.1. Residual Learning
Let us considerH(x)as an underlying mapping to be
ﬁt by a few stacked layers (not necessarily the entire net),
withxdenoting the inputs to the ﬁrst of these layers. If one
hypothesizes that multiple nonlinear layers can asymptoti-
cally approximate complicated functions2, then it is equiv-
alent to hypothesize that they can asymptotically approxi-
mate the residual functions, i.e.,H(x)\x00x(assuming that
the input and output are of the same dimensions). So
rather than expect stacked layers to approximate H(x), we
explicitly let these layers approximate a residual function
F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The original function thus becomes
F(x)+x. Although both forms should be able to asymptot-
ically approximate the desired functions (as hypothesized),
the ease of learning might be different.
This reformulation is motivated by the counterintuitive
phenomena about the degradation problem (Fig. 1, left). As
we discussed in the introduction, if the added layers can
be constructed as identity mappings, a deeper model should
have training error no greater than its shallower counter-
part. The degradation problem suggests that the solvers
might have difﬁculties in approximating identity mappings
by multiple nonlinear layers. With the residual learning re-
formulation, if identity mappings are optimal, the solvers
may simply drive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay-
ers toward zero to approach identity mappings.
In real cases, it is unlikely that identity mappings are op-
timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the
problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity
mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the
solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity
mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show
by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual functions in
general have small responses, suggesting that identity map-
pings provide reasonable preconditioning.
3.2. Identity Mapping by Shortcuts
We adopt residual learning to every few stacked layers.
A building block is shown in Fig. 2. Formally, in this paper
we consider a building block deﬁned as:
y=F(x;fWig) +x: (1)
Here xandyare the input and output vectors of the lay-

residual mapping to be learned. For the example in Fig. 2
that has two layers, F=W2\x1b(W1x)in which\x1bdenotes
2This hypothesis, however, is still an open question. See [28].ReLU [29] and the biases are omitted for simplifying no-
tations. The operation F+xis performed by a shortcut
connection and element-wise addition. We adopt the sec-
ond nonlinearity after the addition ( i.e.,\x1b(y), see Fig. 2).
The shortcut connections in Eqn.(1) introduce neither ex-
tra parameter nor computation complexity. This is not only
attractive in practice but also important in our comparisons
between plain and residual networks. We can fairly com-
pare plain/residual networks that simultaneously have the
same number of parameters, depth, width, and computa-
tional cost (except for the negligible element-wise addition).
The dimensions of xandFmust be equal in Eqn.(1).
If this is not the case ( e.g., when changing the input/output
channels), we can perform a linear projection Wsby the
shortcut connections to match the dimensions:
y=F(x;fWig) +Wsx: (2)
We can also use a square matrix Wsin Eqn.(1). But we will
show by experiments that the identity mapping is sufﬁcient
for addressing the degradation problem and is economical,
and thusWsis only used when matching dimensions.
The form of the residual function Fis ﬂexible. Exper-
iments in this paper involve a function Fthat has two or
three layers (Fig. 5), while more layers are possible. But if
Fhas only a single layer, Eqn.(1) is similar to a linear layer:
y=W1x+x, for which we have not observed advantages.
We also note that although the above notations are about
fully-connected layers for simplicity, they are applicable to
convolutional layers. The function F(x;fWig)can repre-
sent multiple convolutional layers. The element-wise addi-
tion is performed on two feature maps, channel by channel.
3.3. Network Architectures
We have tested various plain/residual nets, and have ob-
served consistent phenomena. To provide instances for dis-
cussion, we describe two models for ImageNet as follows.
Plain Network. Our plain baselines (Fig. 3, middle) are
mainly inspired by the philosophy of VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3,
left). The convolutional layers mostly have 3 \x023 ﬁlters and
follow two simple design rules: (i) for the same output
feature map size, the layers have the same number of ﬁl-
ters; and (ii) if the feature map size is halved, the num-
ber of ﬁlters is doubled so as to preserve the time com-
plexity per layer. We perform downsampling directly by
convolutional layers that have a stride of 2. The network
ends with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way
fully-connected layer with softmax. The total number of
weighted layers is 34 in Fig. 3 (middle).
It is worth noticing that our model has fewer ﬁlters and
lower complexity than VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3, left). Our 34-
layer baseline has 3.6 billion FLOPs (multiply-adds), which
is only 18% of VGG-19 (19.6 billion FLOPs).

of the 18-layer plain network is a subspace of that of the

We argue that this optimization difﬁculty is unlikely to
be caused by vanishing gradients. These plain networks are
trained with BN [16], which ensures forward propagated
signals to have non-zero variances. We also verify that the
backward propagated gradients exhibit healthy norms with
BN. So neither forward nor backward signals vanish. In
fact, the 34-layer plain net is still able to achieve compet-
itive accuracy (Table 3), suggesting that the solver works
to some extent. We conjecture that the deep plain nets may

mization difﬁculties will be studied in the future.
Residual Networks. Next we evaluate 18-layer and 34-

are the same as the above plain nets, expect that a shortcut

(right). In the ﬁrst comparison (Table 2 and Fig. 4 right),

response:
The identity shortcut in ResNet bottleneck architecture is a way of reducing the number of parameters in the network without affecting its performance. This means that the network has no extra parameter compared to the plain counterpart, which is advantageous for optimization ease and model complexity. The 34-layer ResNet exhibits considerably lower training error and is generalizable to the validation data, indicating that the degradation problem is well addressed in this setting. However, compared to its plain counterpart, the 34-layer ResNet still has a higher training error due to the degrade-ment problem. This suggests that optimization ease and model complexity are not solely determined by projection shortcuts but also by the identity shortcut in ResNet bottleneck architecture.

reference: In ResNet bottleneck architectures, identity shortcuts are particularly important because they do not increase the time complexity or model size, unlike projection shortcuts. Specifically, replacing the identity shortcut with a projection doubles both time complexity and model size since the shortcut connects high-dimensional ends. The identity shortcuts thus enable more efficient models, reducing memory and time complexity while maintaining training effectiveness. This design choice helps ease optimization by providing faster convergence at the early stages, even for nets that are not overly deep (such as an 18-layer net). Additionally, while plain deep networks suffer from increased training error as depth increases due to fundamental optimization difficulties, ResNets, which employ identity shortcuts in their residual blocks, overcome these difficulties, demonstrating improved accuracy gains with increased depth. This is evidenced by experiments on CIFAR-10 where ResNets show consistent convergence and lower errors even at depths like 110 layers. Therefore, the use of identity shortcuts in ResNet bottleneck designs both reduces complexity and improves optimization, enabling very deep networks to be effectively trained.

user_input: How did the use of R-CNN and ensemble networks contribute to winning the ILSVRC 2015 localization task?
retrieved_contexts:
['A. Object Detection Baselines
In this section we introduce our detection method based
on the baseline Faster R-CNN [32] system. The models are
initialized by the ImageNet classiﬁcation models, and then
ﬁne-tuned on the object detection data. We have experi-
mented with ResNet-50/101 at the time of the ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 detection competitions.
Unlike VGG-16 used in [32], our ResNet has no hidden
fc layers. We adopt the idea of “Networks on Conv fea-
ture maps” (NoC) [33] to address this issue. We compute
the full-image shared conv feature maps using those lay-
ers whose strides on the image are no greater than 16 pixels
(i.e., conv1, conv2 x, conv3 x, and conv4 x, totally 91 conv
layers in ResNet-101; Table 1). We consider these layers as
analogous to the 13 conv layers in VGG-16, and by doing
so, both ResNet and VGG-16 have conv feature maps of the
same total stride (16 pixels). These layers are shared by a
region proposal network (RPN, generating 300 proposals)
[32] and a Fast R-CNN detection network [7]. RoI pool-
ing [7] is performed before conv5 1. On this RoI-pooled
feature, all layers of conv5 x and up are adopted for each
region, playing the roles of VGG-16’s fc layers. The ﬁnal
classiﬁcation layer is replaced by two sibling layers (classi-
ﬁcation and box regression [7]).
For the usage of BN layers, after pre-training, we com-
pute the BN statistics (means and variances) for each layer
on the ImageNet training set. Then the BN layers are ﬁxed
during ﬁne-tuning for object detection. As such, the BN
layers become linear activations with constant offsets and
scales, and BN statistics are not updated by ﬁne-tuning. We
ﬁx the BN layers mainly for reducing memory consumption
in Faster R-CNN training.
PASCAL VOC
Following [7, 32], for the PASCAL VOC 2007 testset,
we use the 5k trainval images in VOC 2007 and 16k train-
valimages in VOC 2012 for training (“07+12”). For the
PASCAL VOC 2012 testset, we use the 10k trainval +test
images in VOC 2007 and 16k trainval images in VOC 2012
for training (“07++12”). The hyper-parameters for train-
ing Faster R-CNN are the same as in [32]. Table 7 shows
the results. ResNet-101 improves the mAP by >3% over
VGG-16. This gain is solely because of the improved fea-
tures learned by ResNet.
MS COCO
The MS COCO dataset [26] involves 80 object cate-
gories. We evaluate the PASCAL VOC metric (mAP @
IoU = 0.5) and the standard COCO metric (mAP @ IoU =.5:.05:.95). We use the 80k images on the train set for train-
ing and the 40k images on the val set for evaluation. Our
detection system for COCO is similar to that for PASCAL
VOC. We train the COCO models with an 8-GPU imple-
mentation, and thus the RPN step has a mini-batch size of8 images ( i.e., 1 per GPU) and the Fast R-CNN step has a
mini-batch size of 16 images. The RPN step and Fast R-
CNN step are both trained for 240k iterations with a learn-
ing rate of 0.001 and then for 80k iterations with 0.0001.
Table 8 shows the results on the MS COCO validation
set. ResNet-101 has a 6% increase of mAP@[.5,.95] over
VGG-16, which is a 28% relative improvement, solely con-
tributed by the features learned by the better network. Re-
markably, the mAP@[.5,.95]’s absolute increase (6.0%) is
nearly as big as mAP@.5’s (6.9%). This suggests that a
deeper network can improve both recognition and localiza-
tion.
B. Object Detection Improvements
For completeness, we report the improvements made for
the competitions. These improvements are based on deep
features and thus should beneﬁt from residual learning.
MS COCO
Box reﬁnement. Our box reﬁnement partially follows the it-
erative localization in [6]. In Faster R-CNN, the ﬁnal output
is a regressed box that is different from its proposal box. So
for inference, we pool a new feature from the regressed box
and obtain a new classiﬁcation score and a new regressed
box. We combine these 300 new predictions with the orig-
inal 300 predictions. Non-maximum suppression (NMS) is
applied on the union set of predicted boxes using an IoU
threshold of 0.3 [8], followed by box voting [6]. Box re-
ﬁnement improves mAP by about 2 points (Table 9).
Global context. We combine global context in the Fast
R-CNN step. Given the full-image conv feature map, we
pool a feature by global Spatial Pyramid Pooling [12] (with
a “single-level” pyramid) which can be implemented as
“RoI” pooling using the entire image’s bounding box as the
RoI. This pooled feature is fed into the post-RoI layers to
obtain a global context feature. This global feature is con-
catenated with the original per-region feature, followed by
the sibling classiﬁcation and box regression layers. This
new structure is trained end-to-end. Global context im-
proves mAP@.5 by about 1 point (Table 9).
Multi-scale testing. In the above, all results are obtained by
single-scale training/testing as in [32], where the image’s

The use of ResNet and an ensemble network (Faster R-CNN) contributed significantly to winning the ILSVRC 2015 localization task in COCO 2015. The ResNet was designed to learn region proposals, while the Faster R-CNN was designed to classify and propose regions simultaneously. By combining these two techniques, the system was able to achieve a top-5 localization accuracy of 83.8% on PASSCAL VOC 2012, which is 10 points higher than the previous best result in the detection task in COCO 2015. The use of an ensemble network allowed for better performance and reduced the error compared to using a single model.

response:
The question regarding how improvements in object detection performance on the MS COCO dataset, specifically the 6.0% increase in mAP@[.5, .95] achieved by replacing VGG-16 with ResNet-101 and the box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing techniques demonstrates that deep residual networks (RNs) are able to produce higher training error than shallower architectures. RNs have been shown to be more easily optimized by construction of a solution that produces no higher training error than its shallower counterpart. This suggests that the improvements in object detection performance on the MS COCO dataset, specifically the 6.0% increase in mAP@[.5, .95], are not due to overﬁtting or simply using shallower architectures. Instead, RNs have been shown to be able to produce higher training error than shallow architectures, which suggests that deeper networks with RNs can achieve better performance in visual recognition tasks.

📄 Sources:

reference: The object detection performance improvements on the MS COCO dataset demonstrate the advantages of deep residual networks through multiple factors. First, replacing VGG-16 with ResNet-101 yields a 6.0% absolute increase in mAP@[.5, .95], which corresponds to a 28% relative improvement solely due to the better learned representations of the deeper network. This suggests that deeper residual networks improve both recognition and localization capabilities. Moreover, additional refinements such as box refinement improve mAP by about 2 points by combining new predictions with original proposals followed by non-maximum suppression and box voting. Incorporating global context through pooling the entire image’s convolutional feature map and concatenating it with per-region features further improves mAP@.5 by around 1 point. Multi-scale testing, involving computation of convolutional feature maps on an image pyramid with varying shorter side lengths, raises mAP@[.5, .95] and mAP@.5 even further. These techniques together underline how deep residual networks, exemplified by ResNet-101, combined with advanced detection strategies, significantly enhance object detection results on the challenging MS COCO benchmark.

In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-
ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19])
without modifying the solvers.
We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet
[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-
sults substantially better than previous networks.
Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-
layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on
ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG
nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on theImageNet testset, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC
2015 classiﬁcation competition. The extremely deep rep-
resentations also have excellent generalization performance
on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the
1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization,
COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].
These gates are data-dependent and have parameters, in
contrast to our identity shortcuts that are parameter-free.
When a gated shortcut is “closed” (approaching zero), the
layers in highway networks represent non-residual func-
tions. On the contrary, our formulation always learns
residual functions; our identity shortcuts are never closed,
and all information is always passed through, with addi-
tional residual functions to be learned. In addition, high-
2', 'way networks have not demonstrated accuracy gains with
extremely increased depth ( e.g., over 100 layers).
3. Deep Residual Learning
3.1. Residual Learning
Let us considerH(x)as an underlying mapping to be
ﬁt by a few stacked layers (not necessarily the entire net),
withxdenoting the inputs to the ﬁrst of these layers. If one
hypothesizes that multiple nonlinear layers can asymptoti-
cally approximate complicated functions2, then it is equiv-
alent to hypothesize that they can asymptotically approxi-

the input and output are of the same dimensions). So
rather than expect stacked layers to approximate H(x), we
explicitly let these layers approximate a residual function
F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The original function thus becomes
F(x)+x. Although both forms should be able to asymptot-
ically approximate the desired functions (as hypothesized),
the ease of learning might be different.
This reformulation is motivated by the counterintuitive
phenomena about the degradation problem (Fig. 1, left). As
we discussed in the introduction, if the added layers can
be constructed as identity mappings, a deeper model should
have training error no greater than its shallower counter-
part. The degradation problem suggests that the solvers
might have difﬁculties in approximating identity mappings
by multiple nonlinear layers. With the residual learning re-
formulation, if identity mappings are optimal, the solvers
may simply drive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay-
ers toward zero to approach identity mappings.
In real cases, it is unlikely that identity mappings are op-
timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the
problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity
mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the
solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity
mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show
by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual functions in
general have small responses, suggesting that identity map-
pings provide reasonable preconditioning.

attractive in practice but also important in our comparisons
between plain and residual networks. We can fairly com-
pare plain/residual networks that simultaneously have the
same number of parameters, depth, width, and computa-
tional cost (except for the negligible element-wise addition).
The dimensions of xandFmust be equal in Eqn.(1).
If this is not the case ( e.g., when changing the input/output
channels), we can perform a linear projection Wsby the
shortcut connections to match the dimensions:

We can also use a square matrix Wsin Eqn.(1). But we will

The form of the residual function Fis ﬂexible. Exper-

We have tested various plain/residual nets, and have ob-

cussion, we describe two models for ImageNet as follows.
Plain Network. Our plain baselines (Fig. 3, middle) are
mainly inspired by the philosophy of VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3,
left). The convolutional layers mostly have 3 \x023 ﬁlters and
follow two simple design rules: (i) for the same output
feature map size, the layers have the same number of ﬁl-

ber of ﬁlters is doubled so as to preserve the time com-
plexity per layer. We perform downsampling directly by
convolutional layers that have a stride of 2. The network
ends with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way
fully-connected layer with softmax. The total number of
weighted layers is 34 in Fig. 3 (middle).
It is worth noticing that our model has fewer ﬁlters and

response:
The architectures of VGG net and deep residual networks (DNNs) have been compared in terms of their complexity and performance on various visual recognition tasks. In terms of complexity, the VGG net has fewer layers and fewer filters per layer than DNNs, which can result in a lower number of parameters to train and a smaller computational footprint. However, the DNNs have more layers and more filters per layer, which can lead to better generalization capabilities and higher accuracy on complex tasks.

In terms of performance, both VGG net and DNNs have been shown to perform well on various visual recognition tasks such as image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation. The VGG net has been particularly successful in these tasks due to its simplicity and ability to extract feature maps from different layers. However, the DNNs have been shown to outperform the VGG net in some cases, especially when dealing with complex datasets or tasks that require more advanced features.

Overall, while both VGG net and DNNs have their advantages and disadvantages, it is clear that they can be used together to achieve better results on various visual recognition tasks.

reference: The architectures of VGG nets and deep residual networks differ significantly in terms of complexity and performance on visual recognition tasks. VGG nets, specifically VGG-19, have a high computational complexity with 19.6 billion FLOPs (multiply-adds), whereas the 34-layer plain network inspired by VGG nets has only 3.6 billion FLOPs, which is 18% of VGG-19's complexity. In contrast, deep residual networks, which can have a depth of up to 152 layers, are designed to be easier to optimize and can achieve higher accuracy due to their increased depth. Despite being 8 times deeper than VGG nets, these residual networks maintain lower complexity. On the ImageNet dataset, an ensemble of residual nets achieved a 3.57% error rate, winning the 1st place in the ILSVRC 2015 classification task. Additionally, the deep residual networks demonstrated excellent generalization performance, securing 1st places in various tasks such as ImageNet detection, localization, COCO detection, and segmentation in the ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions. This indicates that while VGG nets are computationally intensive, deep residual networks offer superior performance and efficiency in visual recognition tasks.

['Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
Kaiming He Xiangyu Zhang Shaoqing Ren Jian Sun
Microsoft Research
fkahe, v-xiangz, v-shren, jiansun g@microsoft.com
Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
1. Introduction
Deep convolutional neural networks [22, 21] have led
to a series of breakthroughs for image classiﬁcation [21,
50, 40]. Deep networks naturally integrate low/mid/high-
level features [50] and classiﬁers in an end-to-end multi-
layer fashion, and the “levels” of features can be enriched
by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence
[41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance,
and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging
ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models,
with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Many other non-
trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

has higher training error, and thus test error. Similar phenomena
on ImageNet is presented in Fig. 4.
greatly beneﬁted from very deep models.
Driven by the signiﬁcance of depth, a question arises: Is
learning better networks as easy as stacking more layers?
An obstacle to answering this question was the notorious
problem of vanishing/exploding gradients [1, 9], which
hamper convergence from the beginning. This problem,
however, has been largely addressed by normalized initial-
ization [23, 9, 37, 13] and intermediate normalization layers
[16], which enable networks with tens of layers to start con-
verging for stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with back-
propagation [22].
When deeper networks are able to start converging, a
degradation problem has been exposed: with the network
depth increasing, accuracy gets saturated (which might be
unsurprising) and then degrades rapidly. Unexpectedly,
such degradation is not caused by overﬁtting, and adding
more layers to a suitably deep model leads to higher train-
ing error, as reported in [11, 42] and thoroughly veriﬁed by
our experiments. Fig. 1 shows a typical example.
The degradation (of training accuracy) indicates that not
all systems are similarly easy to optimize. Let us consider a
shallower architecture and its deeper counterpart that adds
more layers onto it. There exists a solution by construction
to the deeper model: the added layers are identity mapping,
and the other layers are copied from the learned shallower
model. The existence of this constructed solution indicates
that a deeper model should produce no higher training error
than its shallower counterpart. But experiments show that
our current solvers on hand are unable to ﬁnd solutions that

are comparably good or better than the constructed solution
(or unable to do so in feasible time).
In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-
ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19])
without modifying the solvers.
We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet
[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-
sults substantially better than previous networks.
Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-
layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on
ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG
nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on theImageNet testset, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC
2015 classiﬁcation competition. The extremely deep rep-
resentations also have excellent generalization performance
on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the
1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization,
COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].
These gates are data-dependent and have parameters, in
contrast to our identity shortcuts that are parameter-free.
When a gated shortcut is “closed” (approaching zero), the
layers in highway networks represent non-residual func-
tions. On the contrary, our formulation always learns
residual functions; our identity shortcuts are never closed,
and all information is always passed through, with addi-
tional residual functions to be learned. In addition, high-

As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we
randomly sample 224 \x02224 crops for data augmentation.
We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To

domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive
and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing,
the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally.

response:
The use of deep residual networks (RNNs) contributed to the success in the ILSVRC 2015 classification and localization tasks by improving the representation power of the network. RNNs are a type of recurrent neural network that can capture long-term dependencies in data, which is beneficial for image recognition since images often contain complex patterns and relationships between different objects or features. By using deep RNNs, the network can learn to recognize more complex patterns and relationships, resulting in better classification and localization results.

In terms of the R-CNN implementation, it played a crucial role in achieving top localization results. The R-CNN is a type of convolutional neural network (CNN) that uses residual connections to improve the representation power of the network. Residual connections are a technique where the output of one layer is added back to the input of the next layer, instead of directly feeding the output of the previous layer into the next layer. This allows the network to learn to capture long-term dependencies and generate more accurate representations. By using residual connections in the R-CNN, the network can better capture the relationships between different objects or features in the image, resulting in better localization results.

In summary, the use of deep RNNs and residual connections in the R-CNN implementation contributed to the success in the ILSVRC 2015 classification and localization tasks by improving the representation power of the network and generating more accurate representations for image recognition. The R-CNN implementation played a crucial role in achieving top localization results.

reference: The use of deep residual networks significantly contributed to the success in the ILSVRC 2015 classification task by allowing the training of networks with a depth of up to 152 layers, which is 8 times deeper than VGG nets, while maintaining lower complexity. This approach led to a 3.57% error on the ImageNet test set, securing 1st place in the classification task. Additionally, the residual learning framework proved to be generic, providing excellent generalization performance across various recognition tasks, including ImageNet detection, localization, COCO detection, and segmentation, where it also won 1st places. In the localization task, the R-CNN implementation played a crucial role by using a RoI-centric approach instead of Fast R-CNN, which was less effective due to highly overlapping proposal regions. The R-CNN method involved predicting bounding boxes for ground truth classes using the per-class RPN, extracting the highest scored 200 proposals as training samples, and fine-tuning the network on these samples. This approach reduced the top-5 localization error to 10.6% on the validation set and further to 9.0% on the test set using an ensemble of networks, achieving a 64% relative reduction of error compared to ILSVRC 2014 results and winning 1st place in the localization task.

C. ImageNet Localization
The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to
classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we
assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for
predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza-
tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes
based on the predicted classes. We adopt the “per-class re-
gression” (PCR) strategy [40, 41], learning a bounding box
regressor for each class. We pre-train the networks for Im-
ageNet classiﬁcation and then ﬁne-tune them for localiza-
tion. We train networks on the provided 1000-class Ima-
geNet training set.
Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame-
work of [32] with a few modiﬁcations. Unlike the way in
[32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is
designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib-
ling 1\x021 convolutional layers for binary classiﬁcation ( cls)
and box regression ( reg), as in [32]. The clsandreglayers
are both in a per-class from, in contrast to [32]. Speciﬁ-
cally, the clslayer has a 1000-d output, and each dimension
isbinary logistic regression for predicting being or not be-
ing an object class; the reglayer has a 1000\x024-d output
consisting of box regressors for 1000 classes. As in [32],
our bounding box regression is with reference to multiple
translation-invariant “anchor” boxes at each position.
As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we
randomly sample 224 \x02224 crops for data augmentation.
We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To
avoid negative samples being dominate, 8 anchors are ran-
domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive
and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing,
the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally.

by this, in our current experiment we use the original R-

per-class RPN trained as above on the training images to
predict bounding boxes for the ground truth class. These

Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
1. Introduction
Deep convolutional neural networks [22, 21] have led

50, 40]. Deep networks naturally integrate low/mid/high-

layer fashion, and the “levels” of features can be enriched
by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence
[41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance,
and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging
ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models,
with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Many other non-
trivial visual recognition tasks [8, 12, 7, 32, 27] have also

The RPN (Region Proposal Network) framework plays a crucial role in reducing localization error in the ImageNet task. The RPN framework is designed to generate region proposals, which are small, non-overlapping regions of interest (ROIs) that contain at least one object of interest. By generating multiple proposals for each image, the RPN framework can reduce the number of false positives and improve the overall accuracy of the detection algorithm.

In the ImageNet task, the RPN framework is used to generate region proposals for each image. The generated proposals are then fed into a classification head to classify each proposal as either a background or an object of interest. This process reduces the number of false positives and improves the overall accuracy of the detection algorithm.

In summary, the RPN framework plays a crucial role in reducing localization error in the ImageNet task by generating region proposals that are more accurate and less prone to false positives.

This approach allows for a more precise prediction of bounding boxes for each class, significantly reducing the center-crop error to 13.3% compared to VGG's 33.1%. Additionally, using dense and multi-scale testing further reduces the error to 11.7%. The RPN framework, when combined with R-CNN, reduces the top-5 localization error to 10.6% on the validation set and 9.0% on the test set, showcasing a 64% relative reduction of error compared to previous methods.

3. Deep Residual Learning
3.1. Residual Learning
Let us considerH(x)as an underlying mapping to be
ﬁt by a few stacked layers (not necessarily the entire net),
withxdenoting the inputs to the ﬁrst of these layers. If one
hypothesizes that multiple nonlinear layers can asymptoti-
cally approximate complicated functions2, then it is equiv-
alent to hypothesize that they can asymptotically approxi-
mate the residual functions, i.e.,H(x)\x00x(assuming that
the input and output are of the same dimensions). So
rather than expect stacked layers to approximate H(x), we
explicitly let these layers approximate a residual function
F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The original function thus becomes
F(x)+x. Although both forms should be able to asymptot-
ically approximate the desired functions (as hypothesized),
the ease of learning might be different.
This reformulation is motivated by the counterintuitive
phenomena about the degradation problem (Fig. 1, left). As
we discussed in the introduction, if the added layers can
be constructed as identity mappings, a deeper model should
have training error no greater than its shallower counter-
part. The degradation problem suggests that the solvers
might have difﬁculties in approximating identity mappings
by multiple nonlinear layers. With the residual learning re-
formulation, if identity mappings are optimal, the solvers
may simply drive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay-
ers toward zero to approach identity mappings.
In real cases, it is unlikely that identity mappings are op-
timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the
problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity
mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the
solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity
mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show
by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual functions in
general have small responses, suggesting that identity map-
pings provide reasonable preconditioning.
3.2. Identity Mapping by Shortcuts
We adopt residual learning to every few stacked layers.

3.3. Network Architectures
We have tested various plain/residual nets, and have ob-
served consistent phenomena. To provide instances for dis-
cussion, we describe two models for ImageNet as follows.
Plain Network. Our plain baselines (Fig. 3, middle) are
mainly inspired by the philosophy of VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3,
left). The convolutional layers mostly have 3 \x023 ﬁlters and
follow two simple design rules: (i) for the same output
feature map size, the layers have the same number of ﬁl-
ters; and (ii) if the feature map size is halved, the num-
ber of ﬁlters is doubled so as to preserve the time com-
plexity per layer. We perform downsampling directly by
convolutional layers that have a stride of 2. The network
ends with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way
fully-connected layer with softmax. The total number of
weighted layers is 34 in Fig. 3 (middle).
It is worth noticing that our model has fewer ﬁlters and
lower complexity than VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3, left). Our 34-
layer baseline has 3.6 billion FLOPs (multiply-adds), which
is only 18% of VGG-19 (19.6 billion FLOPs).

In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x):=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+x. We hypothesize that it
is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize
the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an
identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push
the residual to zero than to ﬁt an identity mapping by a stack
of nonlinear layers.
The formulation of F(x)+xcan be realized by feedfor-
ward neural networks with “shortcut connections” (Fig. 2).
Shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] are those skipping one or
more layers. In our case, the shortcut connections simply
perform identity mapping, and their outputs are added to
the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short-
cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa-
tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained
end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas-
ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19])
without modifying the solvers.
We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet
[36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our
method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets
are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that
simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the
depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy
accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re-
sults substantially better than previous networks.
Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set
[20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the
effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset.
We present successfully trained models on this dataset with
over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers.
On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain
excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152-
layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on
ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG
nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on theImageNet testset, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC
2015 classiﬁcation competition. The extremely deep rep-
resentations also have excellent generalization performance
on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the
1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization,
COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].

response:
Figure 7 (left) demonstrates the effective use of residual learning in addressing the degradation problem in deep networks. The figure shows that when the depth of a network is increased, the initial learning rate used to warm up training is too large and causes the training error to increase significantly. However, by using a smaller learning rate (0.1) and continuously decreasing it until convergence, the network can converge more effectively and achieve better results. This demonstrates that residual learning is an effective technique for addressing the degradation problem in deep networks.

reference: Fig. 7 demonstrates the effectiveness of residual learning in addressing the degradation problem by showing that the learned residual functions generally have small responses. This suggests that identity mappings provide reasonable preconditioning, making it easier for solvers to find perturbations with reference to an identity mapping rather than learning the function as a new one. The residual learning reformulation helps in preconditioning the problem, which is crucial because if the optimal function is closer to an identity mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the solver to approximate it. This approach is supported by experiments that indicate the residual functions' small responses, thereby validating the hypothesis that identity mappings can effectively address the degradation problem in deep networks.

7', 'A. Object Detection Baselines
In this section we introduce our detection method based
on the baseline Faster R-CNN [32] system. The models are
initialized by the ImageNet classiﬁcation models, and then
ﬁne-tuned on the object detection data. We have experi-
mented with ResNet-50/101 at the time of the ILSVRC &
COCO 2015 detection competitions.
Unlike VGG-16 used in [32], our ResNet has no hidden
fc layers. We adopt the idea of “Networks on Conv fea-
ture maps” (NoC) [33] to address this issue. We compute
the full-image shared conv feature maps using those lay-
ers whose strides on the image are no greater than 16 pixels
(i.e., conv1, conv2 x, conv3 x, and conv4 x, totally 91 conv
layers in ResNet-101; Table 1). We consider these layers as
analogous to the 13 conv layers in VGG-16, and by doing
so, both ResNet and VGG-16 have conv feature maps of the
same total stride (16 pixels). These layers are shared by a
region proposal network (RPN, generating 300 proposals)
[32] and a Fast R-CNN detection network [7]. RoI pool-
ing [7] is performed before conv5 1. On this RoI-pooled
feature, all layers of conv5 x and up are adopted for each
region, playing the roles of VGG-16’s fc layers. The ﬁnal
classiﬁcation layer is replaced by two sibling layers (classi-
ﬁcation and box regression [7]).
For the usage of BN layers, after pre-training, we com-
pute the BN statistics (means and variances) for each layer
on the ImageNet training set. Then the BN layers are ﬁxed
during ﬁne-tuning for object detection. As such, the BN
layers become linear activations with constant offsets and
scales, and BN statistics are not updated by ﬁne-tuning. We
ﬁx the BN layers mainly for reducing memory consumption
in Faster R-CNN training.
PASCAL VOC
Following [7, 32], for the PASCAL VOC 2007 testset,
we use the 5k trainval images in VOC 2007 and 16k train-
valimages in VOC 2012 for training (“07+12”). For the
PASCAL VOC 2012 testset, we use the 10k trainval +test
images in VOC 2007 and 16k trainval images in VOC 2012
for training (“07++12”). The hyper-parameters for train-
ing Faster R-CNN are the same as in [32]. Table 7 shows
the results. ResNet-101 improves the mAP by >3% over
VGG-16. This gain is solely because of the improved fea-
tures learned by ResNet.
MS COCO
The MS COCO dataset [26] involves 80 object cate-
gories. We evaluate the PASCAL VOC metric (mAP @
